label: Simple chalazion without vision or orbital red flags
summary: Your symptoms fit a chalazion, a blocked oil gland in the eyelid.
found: Your clinician diagnosed a simple chalazion. We did not find vision changes, severe eye pain, eye injury, or signs of a deeper infection around the eye today.
home: Use warm compresses as your clinician instructed.
Keep the eyelid clean and avoid eye makeup until improving.
Do not squeeze, pop, or cut the eyelid bump.
meds: Use eye medicine only if it was prescribed.
Do not put non-eye medicines or harsh products in the eye.
return: Vision changes, severe eye pain, pain with eye movement, or worsening eye redness.
Fever, rapidly worsening eyelid swelling, drainage, or spreading redness around the eye.
The bump becomes recurrent, persistent, or interferes with vision.
follow: Follow up with primary care, urgent care, optometry, or ophthalmology if symptoms are not improving, recur, or affect vision.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of simple chalazion.
No vision change, severe eye pain, orbital cellulitis concern, eye trauma, contact lens complication, infection concern, recurrent mass, or specialist-directed eye plan.
exclusion: Vision change, severe eye pain, photophobia, pain with eye movement, orbital or periorbital cellulitis concern, or eye trauma.
Contact lens complication, recurrent or persistent eyelid mass, immune compromise, systemic illness, pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed ophthalmology plan.
Medication selection not supplied by clinician.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus and AAO support chalazion/eyelid-bump framing, conservative warm-compress care, avoiding squeezing or popping, and escalation for vision trouble, worsening pain/swelling, infection concern, or persistent/recurrent eyelid mass. Autonomous Phase 1651-1700 approved this narrow v1 only for clinician-diagnosed simple chalazion without vision change, severe eye pain, orbital/periorbital cellulitis concern, eye trauma, contact lens complication, recurrent/persistent mass, immunocompromised/pediatric pathway, systemic illness, or specialist-directed eye plan.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of simple chalazion
no vision change, severe eye pain, eye trauma, or orbital/periorbital cellulitis concern documented
no contact lens complication, recurrent/persistent mass, immunocompromised, pediatric, systemic illness, or specialist-directed eye pathway

label: Uncomplicated tinea pedis without cellulitis or diabetic foot risk
summary: Your symptoms fit athlete's foot, also called tinea pedis.
found: Your clinician diagnosed uncomplicated tinea pedis. We did not find cellulitis, an open infected wound, or diabetic-foot risk requiring a different plan today.
home: Keep the feet clean and dry, especially between the toes.
Change socks when damp and avoid sharing towels or footwear.
Use shower shoes in shared wet areas when possible.
meds: Use antifungal medicine only if it was prescribed or recommended, and follow the label.
Do not use steroid creams on the rash unless your clinician specifically told you to.
return: Spreading redness, warmth, swelling, pus, fever, red streaks, or worsening pain.
Open sores, black skin, numbness, or new foot wound.
Symptoms are spreading, recurrent, or not improving with the plan you were given.
follow: Follow up with primary care, urgent care, dermatology, or podiatry if symptoms are not improving or if this keeps coming back.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated tinea pedis.
No cellulitis, open wound, diabetic-foot pathway, immunocompromised host, nail/scalp/groin pathway, systemic illness, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Cellulitis, abscess, open wound, secondary infection, systemic illness, or rapidly spreading rash.
Diabetes with foot wound/risk, PVD, neuropathy, immunocompromised host, nail/scalp/groin involvement, recurrent/extensive disease, or specialist-directed podiatry/dermatology plan.
Medication selection not supplied by clinician.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus and CDC support athlete's-foot/tinea-pedis framing, keeping feet clean and dry, avoiding shared wet exposure, and escalation for spreading infection or non-improvement. Autonomous Phase 1651-1700 approved this narrow v1 only for clinician-diagnosed uncomplicated tinea pedis without cellulitis, open wound, diabetic-foot/PVD/neuropathy risk, immunocompromised host, nail/scalp/groin or recurrent/extensive pathway, systemic illness, or specialist-directed podiatry/dermatology plan.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated tinea pedis
no cellulitis, secondary infection, diabetic foot, or open foot wound documented
no high-risk host, nail/scalp/groin, recurrent/extensive, systemic illness, or specialist-directed pathway

label: Mild eczema flare without infection or systemic symptoms
summary: Your symptoms fit a mild eczema flare.
found: Your clinician diagnosed a mild eczema flare. We did not find infection, a severe drug rash, or systemic illness today.
home: Use fragrance-free moisturizer often, especially after bathing.
Avoid scratching when possible and keep nails short.
Avoid known triggers such as harsh soaps, fragrances, or irritating fabrics.
meds: Use steroid, anti-itch, or other skin medicine only if prescribed or recommended.
Use acetaminophen or ibuprofen only if you can take it safely and follow the label.
return: Fever, spreading redness, warmth, pus, red streaks, or rapidly worsening pain.
Blistering, skin peeling, mouth sores, eye symptoms, or target-like rash.
The rash becomes widespread, severe, or does not improve with the plan you were given.
follow: Follow up with primary care or dermatology if symptoms are not improving, recur often, or require ongoing medicine adjustment.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of mild eczema or atopic dermatitis flare.
No secondary infection, severe/widespread flare, systemic illness, severe cutaneous adverse reaction, eye/genital involvement, immunocompromised host, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Cellulitis, abscess, pus, fever, secondary infection, severe pain, or rapidly spreading rash.
Skin peeling, blistering, mucosal lesions, eye/genital involvement, severe/widespread flare, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed dermatology plan.
Medication selection not supplied by clinician.
must_not_miss: Stevens-Johnson syndrome or toxic epidermal necrolysis.
Anaphylaxis or severe allergic reaction mimic.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports eczema/atopic-dermatitis flare framing, moisturizer and trigger-avoidance care, prescribed medication adherence, and escalation for infection signs, severe/widespread rash, or systemic symptoms. Autonomous Phase 1651-1700 approved this narrow v1 only for clinician-diagnosed mild eczema flare without secondary infection, severe/widespread flare, mucosal/eye/genital involvement, severe drug-rash concern, systemic illness, pregnancy/pediatric/immunocompromised pathway, or specialist-directed dermatology plan.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of mild eczema or atopic dermatitis flare
no infection, fever, systemic symptoms, mucosal/eye/genital involvement, or severe cutaneous adverse reaction documented
no high-risk host, pregnancy, pediatric, severe/widespread, or specialist-directed pathway

label: Localized oral herpes labialis without eye involvement or immunocompromised host
summary: Your symptoms fit a cold sore, also called oral herpes labialis.
found: Your clinician diagnosed localized oral herpes labialis. We did not find eye involvement, genital involvement, disseminated disease, or immunocompromised-host risk today.
home: Avoid touching the sore except when cleaning the area or applying medicine.
Wash your hands after touching the area.
Avoid kissing, oral sex, or sharing lip products while sores are active.
meds: Use antiviral medicine only if prescribed and exactly as directed.
Use acetaminophen or ibuprofen only if you can take it safely and follow the label.
return: Eye pain, eye redness, vision change, or sores near the eye.
Fever, confusion, severe headache, widespread rash, or rapidly spreading sores.
Symptoms occur in a newborn exposure context, pregnancy concern, or immunocompromised host.
follow: Follow up with primary care, urgent care, dermatology, or ophthalmology as instructed, especially if outbreaks recur or symptoms are not improving.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of localized oral herpes labialis or cold sore.
No eye involvement, genital involvement, disseminated disease, CNS/systemic illness, pregnancy/newborn exposure, immunocompromised host, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Eye pain, red eye, vision change, vesicles near eye, genital lesions, disseminated rash, severe headache, confusion, fever, or systemic illness.
Pregnancy, newborn exposure, immunocompromised host, severe eczema flare/eczema herpeticum concern, or specialist-directed antiviral/ophthalmology/dermatology plan.
Medication selection not supplied by clinician.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports cold sore/oral HSV framing, hand hygiene, avoiding direct contact/sharing during active lesions, prescribed antiviral use when directed, and escalation for eye symptoms, widespread/systemic disease, pregnancy/newborn exposure, or immunocompromised-host context. Autonomous Phase 1651-1700 approved this narrow v1 only for clinician-diagnosed localized oral herpes labialis without eye/genital involvement, disseminated disease, CNS/systemic illness, pregnancy/newborn exposure, immunocompromised host, or specialist-directed plan.

label: Uncomplicated heat rash without heat illness or infection
found: Your clinician diagnosed uncomplicated heat rash. We did not find heat exhaustion, heat stroke, dehydration, or skin infection today.
home: Move to a cooler environment and avoid overheating while the rash heals.
Keep the skin cool and dry.
Wear loose, breathable clothing when possible.
meds: Use skin medicine only if prescribed or recommended.
Use acetaminophen or ibuprofen only if you can take it safely and follow the label.
return: Confusion, fainting, severe weakness, vomiting, dehydration, or symptoms after heat exposure that feel severe.
Fever, spreading redness, warmth, pus, red streaks, or worsening pain.
The rash becomes widespread, blistering, or does not improve after cooling measures.
follow: Follow up with primary care or urgent care if symptoms are not improving or if the rash keeps coming back.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated heat rash.
No heat illness, dehydration, secondary infection, severe/widespread rash, systemic illness, high-risk host pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Heat exhaustion, heat stroke, syncope, confusion, dehydration, persistent vomiting, or unstable vital signs.
Cellulitis, abscess, pus, fever, severe pain, blistering/peeling rash, severe cutaneous adverse reaction, or systemic illness.
Pregnancy, pediatric pathway, immunocompromised host, or specialist-directed dermatology plan.
source_audit_notes: CDC supports heat-rash framing and cooling/drying measures while MedlinePlus supports heat-illness red flags. Autonomous Phase 1651-1700 approved this narrow v1 only for clinician-diagnosed uncomplicated heat rash without heat illness, dehydration, secondary infection, severe/widespread or blistering/peeling rash, systemic illness, pregnancy/pediatric/immunocompromised pathway, or specialist-directed dermatology plan.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated heat rash
